title: Open-Core Distribution
compiled_truth:
Open-core distribution is the strategy of releasing a free, open-source core product to drive broad adoption while monetizing through proprietary enterprise features, support contracts, and managed services layered on top. For infrastructure companies — especially those building low-level primitives around compression, authentication, or transmission — the model is almost uniquely well-suited. The core becomes a trust surface: engineers can audit, fork, and extend it, which lowers the barrier to integration in security-sensitive environments. The enterprise layer then captures value from organizations that need SLAs, audit logging, multi-tenancy, or compliance tooling that no one wants to build themselves.

The thesis matters for Anchor Codec because the infrastructure space is littered with projects that achieved massive adoption but failed to monetize, and conversely, with companies that locked down too much too early and never reached critical mass. The tension is real. [Vellum Systems](companies/vellum-systems) is probably the clearest example in our orbit: they open-sourced their deterministic verification library in early 2027, watched it get pulled into dozens of pipelines within months, and then struggled to draw a clean line between the free tier and their paid orchestration layer. Their community started re-implementing enterprise features in contrib repos, which forced a licensing pivot mid-year. It was messy.

[Keel Networks](companies/keel-networks) took the opposite approach — tight core, generous API surface, enterprise upsell from day one. [Tal Kimura](people/tal-kimura) has spoken publicly about how Keel's early decision to keep the transmission optimizer proprietary actually slowed initial adoption but produced stickier enterprise contracts. Whether that tradeoff was worth it depends on your time horizon and your burn rate.

[Fenn Adeyemi](people/fenn-adeyemi) wrote a widely-circulated internal memo in late 2027 arguing that open-core only works when the boundary between free and paid aligns with a natural "complexity cliff" — the point where self-service stops scaling and organizations genuinely need help. If you draw the line arbitrarily, engineers resent you. If you draw it at a real inflection point, they thank you for it.

One underappreciated detail: open-core distribution isn't just a pricing model, it's a hiring funnel. Contributors become candidates. Contributor become advocates. The compounding effects are hard to measure but show up in recruiter conversion rates and inbound pipeline. The risk, always, is that you're subsidizing your competitors' R&D. But if your core is genuinely hard to replicate at the systems level — as single-pass primitives tend to be — the moat holds longer than most people expect.
timeline: - **2026-04-12** | [Fenn Adeyemi](people/fenn-adeyemi) publishes early blog post titled "Why We're Open-Sourcing the Core" outlining Anchor Codec's distribution philosophy.
- **2026-08-30** | Internal debate on license choice (MIT vs. BSL vs. Apache 2.0) concludes with Apache 2.0 for the core library.
- **2026-11-15** | [Keel Networks](companies/keel-networks) announces proprietary-first model at a Devcon panel; [Tal Kimura](people/tal-kimura) contrasts it explicitly with open-core approaches.
- **2027-01-22** | [Vellum Systems](companies/vellum-systems) releases their deterministic verification library under MIT license; adoption accelerates quickly.
- **2027-05-09** | Vellum community contributors begin reimplementing enterprise features in unofficial forks, triggering internal licensing review.
- **2027-07-18** | [Vellum Systems](companies/vellum-systems) pivots to BSL for new modules; significant community backlash on forums and social media.
- **2027-10-03** | [Fenn Adeyemi](people/fenn-adeyemi) circulates internal memo on the "complexity cliff" framework for drawing open/paid boundaries.
- **2027-12-11** | Anchor Codec ships first enterprise-only module (audit trail + compliance hooks); zero community pushback due to clear boundary.
- **2028-03-25** | [Tal Kimura](people/tal-kimura) and Fenn co-present at Infra Summit on competing open-core vs. proprietary-first distribution strategies.
- **2028-06-14** | Internal metrics review shows 38% of enterprise leads originated from open-source contributor community; validates distribution thesis.

_facts.name: open-core distribution
_facts.description: Free open-source core driving adoption; enterprise features and support as the revenue layer
